coredata.dc:title: A green facile fluorescent sensor based on APTES-modified carbon quantum dots coupled with molecular imprinting for efficient detection of metronidazole in aqueous environment

coredata.dc:description: Metronidazole (MTZ) as a widely used antibiotic of the nitro-imidazole class to treat infections in both humans and animals, is found to be toxic, mutagenic, carcinogenic and poses a risk to aquatic life and human health via the food chain. An eco-green sustainable and novel APTES-CQDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor was developed with a cost-effective hydrothermal method from APTES and waste orange peels, coupled with molecular imprinting using reverse micro-emulsion for rapid and accurate detection of MTZ in water samples. APTES-CQDs achieved simultaneous carbonization, nitrogen doping, and silica-based passivation, eliminated toxic chemicals, and demonstrated zero waste. The sensor response time was 1 min upon rebinding with MTZ and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was proposed to be charge transfer and static quenching. The APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor had exceptional reproducibility (RSD 3.45 %) and showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards MTZ with a linear concentration range of 0.17–40.00 μM (R2 = 0.997), and the detection limit of 0.05 μM. The imprinting factor (IF) reached 4.67 and recycle efficiency achieved over 88 % up to five cycles. The constructed fluorescent probe demonstrated comparable results to other reported methods, with recoveries of 95.1–108.2 %. The sensor has the advantages of simplicity, excellent selectivity, superior sensitivity, good reproducibility, stability, and recyclability.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of APTES-CQDs@NIPs (a), APTES-CQDs@MIPs after (b) and before washing (c); (B) UV–Vis absorbance spectra of MTZ after four washing of APTES-CQDs@MIPs with methanol; (C) UV absorbance spectra of APTES-CQDs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs; (D) XRD spectra of APTES-CQDs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs; (E) FTIR spectra of APTES-CQDs, APTES-CQDs@NIPs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs.

(C). (The λex/λem wavelengths were 350/437 nm, pH = 6.0, Time = 5 min); UV absorption spectrum of MTZ and fluorescence emission spectrum of APTES-CQDs@MIPs (D); UV absorption spectra of APTES-CQDs@MIPs and MTZ (E); and fluorescence lifetime of APTES-CQDs@MIPs before and after addition of MTZ (F).

(The concentration of structural analogs and ions was 15 μM and 150 μM respectively).

head.ce:title: A green facile fluorescent sensor based on APTES-modified carbon quantum dots coupled with molecular imprinting for efficient detection of metronidazole in aqueous environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metronidazole (MTZ) as a widely used antibiotic of the nitro-imidazole class to treat infections in both humans and animals, is found to be toxic, mutagenic, carcinogenic and poses a risk to aquatic life and human health via the food chain. An eco-green sustainable and novel APTES-CQDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor was developed with a cost-effective hydrothermal method from APTES and waste orange peels, coupled with molecular imprinting using reverse micro-emulsion for rapid and accurate detection of MTZ in water samples. APTES-CQDs achieved simultaneous carbonization, nitrogen doping, and silica-based passivation, eliminated toxic chemicals, and demonstrated zero waste. The sensor response time was 1 min upon rebinding with MTZ and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was proposed to be charge transfer and static quenching. The APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor had exceptional reproducibility (RSD 3.45 %) and showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards MTZ with a linear concentration range of 0.17–40.00 μM (R2 = 0.997), and the detection limit of 0.05 μM. The imprinting factor (IF) reached 4.67 and recycle efficiency achieved over 88 % up to five cycles. The constructed fluorescent probe demonstrated comparable results to other reported methods, with recoveries of 95.1–108.2 %. The sensor has the advantages of simplicity, excellent selectivity, superior sensitivity, good reproducibility, stability, and recyclability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Quantum dots (QDs) are quasi-spherical zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based nanomaterials typically with sizes between 1 and 10 nm [1]. When the radii of these nanoparticles are less than or equal to the excitation Bohr radius, quantization of the energy levels takes place and the quantum process occurs [2]. Conventional semiconductor QD sensors contain toxic heavy metals fluorescent core e.g. cadmium, which requires hazardous chemical stabilizers e.g., thioglycolic acid [3]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are considered one of the new promising fluorescent materials having good aqueous dispersibility, superior chemical and photostability, high tuneable photoluminescence, narrow symmetric emission profile, and excellent surface recognition ability as compared to conventional organic fluorophores and semiconductor QDs. Mostly CQDs are synthesized with chemical or synthetic carbon precursors i.e. tris-hydroxymethyl-aminomethane, citric acid, and purpald (4-amino-3-hydrazino-5-mercapto-1,2,4-triazole) [4–6]. In addition, purpald contains hydrazine and mercapto groups, which are toxic, and hydrazine derivatives are well-known carcinogens and require careful handling. Green CQDs can be synthesized using fruit peels, plant leaves, and fruit juices through carbonization followed by surface functionalized with organic and polymeric compounds [7]. The core of CQDs consists of sp
2
/sp
3 hybridized carbon and the surface comprises different oxygen-containing functional groups i.e. hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and carbonyl (C=O). Polymeric aggregation usually makes up the majority of CQD molecules soluble in water. Furthermore, the reactivity of these surface charges limits applications of CQDs [8,9]. Many efforts have been made to enhance the electronic structure, fluorescence performance, stability, solubility, and specific interactions of CQDs by employing phosphoric acid and sulphuric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA), polyethylene (PEG) as doping, surface functionalization and passivating agents, which are hazardous and toxic to the environment. The stability of CQDs purely relying on surface functional groups from biomass or nitrogen dopants is less robust under harsh conditions, and photostability is often unaddressed or limited [10–13]. There are numerous applications of CQDs in the environment and health domains i.e. biosensing and bioimaging, drug delivery, and detection of antibiotics [8,14,15].

Climate change and industrial waste impose a negative impact on the environment and public health due to growing urbanization, and increasing population leads to an increased demand for antibiotics [16]. Over 50 % of all antibiotics may be released into the environment as a result of inadequate metabolism and usage, ultimately aquatic environment acts as their final reservoir where antibiotics accumulate in coastal sediments and seafood, posing chronic toxic effects on human health [17]. Metronidazole (MTZ) is a widely used antibiotic of the nitro-imidazole class to treat infections caused by anaerobic bacteria and protozoa in both humans and animals [18], which has high photo-stability and solubility in water and low biodegradability and is found to be accumulated in marine habitats such as the Mediterranean, Antarctic, and Red Sea [19]. Due to its persistence in nature, MTZ is found to have toxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic qualities in aquatic environments and poses a risk to aquatic life and human health via the food chain [20]. Research has shown that metronidazole causes acute toxicity to a range of aquatic species i.e. Chlorella sp. and Selenastrum capricornutum [21]. Hence, it is crucial to develop a method for the determination of metronidazole. Although conventional analytical techniques and sensing methods such as liquid chromatography–ion trap mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [22], reverse-phase high-performance liquid chromatography (RP-HPLC) [23], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP/MS) [24], square wave voltammetry (SWV) [25] electrochemical sensing (EC) [26] and aptamer-based colorimetric method [27] have low detection limits and good sensitivity, they have drawbacks such as complex sample preparation, difficult to handle, expensive raw material, time-consuming.

Fluorescence spectrophotometry coupled with molecular imprinting is frequently utilized in drug analysis and detection as its inexpensive, easy to use, and good selectivity and sensitivity [28], therefore, QDs layered by molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) shells gained the attention of researchers, making them ideal fluorescent probes capable of detecting antibiotic residues in different water matrices [16]. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) which have three-dimensional binding sites, are specifically tailored to the molecular volume geometry of functional groups of target molecules [29]. Conventional MIP synthesis involves the use of synthetic chemicals i.e. methacrylic acid (MAA) [30], ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), and 2,2-azobis-2-methylpropionitrile (AIBN) [31] are non-degradable or toxic to the environment. Therefore, to address these potential issues there is an urgent need for a sustainable approach to eliminate the toxic chemicals and simplify multi-step protocols i.e. CQD synthesis doping, passivation, or stabilization (post-synthesis modification) for reducing cost, time, and complexity and demonstrating zero waste which aligns with eco-green practices.

This is the first report of amino-functionalized silica-passivated CQDs@MIPs for the detection of aqueous pollutants (metronidazole) employs the integration of APTES and orange peels in a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis achieving simultaneous in-situ carbonization and functionalization. No prior studies have used APTES-modified CQDs@MIPs for MTZ detection. Molecular imprinting was performed on APTES-CQDs by reverse micro-emulsion method through optimized ratio (1:4:20) of metronidazole (MTZ), 3- aminopropyl-tri ethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) to achieve high selectivity and sensitivity. In addition to optimization of pH, dosage and response time, recyclability and reproducibility of APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor were also examined thoroughly.

Fresh oranges were bought from the local market near Ocean University of China, Qingdao. Metronidazole (MTZ), 2-methylimidazole (2-MIZ), o-phenyldiamine (o-PD), ornidazole (ORZ), secnidazole (SNZ), acetaminophen (ACP), dimetridazole (DMZ), cyclohexane, and Triton X-100 were supplied by Aladdin Corporation (Shanghai, China). Acetone, 3-aminopropyl-tri-ethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ammonia (ammonium hydroxide), and methanol were purchased from Sinopharm Group Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Milli-Q water was used throughout all experiments.

Fluorescence and UV spectra were recorded by the F-2700 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and UV-2500 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) respectively. Surface composition, chemical and crystallographic structure of materials were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific Corporation, Japan) and powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) D-MAX 2500 /PC). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential (ZP) measurements were performed on Malvern Instruments Ltd. (UK). The Brauner-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analysis were performed using a gas sorption analyzer, Autosorb 6100, (Anton Paar Quanta Tec Inc. U.S.A). Fluorescence lifetime was measured using Edinburgh FLS1000 (Britain). CenLee16R tabletop centrifuge (China) was used for centrifugation. Milli-Q water was obtained from the CLW-K10-WP-C Milli-Q (China).

Orange peels (OPs) were washed thoroughly with Milli-Q water and dried in the dark for 10 days, and then were placed in the autoclave at 60 °C for 24 h to remove moisture. Dried orange peels were ground into fine powder by mortar and pestle and stored in a desiccator for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of blue fluorescent APTES-CQDs from orange peels
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon quantum dots were synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method from orange peels and APTES. Details are provided in the Supporting Material.
Molecularly imprinted APTS-CQDs were synthesized by the reverse microemulsion with the modified previous method [32]. Details are provided in the Supporting Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions for APTES-CQDs and APTES CQDs@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize APTES-CQDs with good fluorescence properties and stability, the amount of orange peel powder (OPs) (0.125 g, 0.25 g, 0.5 g, and 1.0 g), the volume of APTES (1 ml, 2 ml, and 3 ml), synthesis time (10 h and 20 h) and synthesis temperature (180 °C and 200 °C) were evaluated. To achieve an excellent fluorescence intensity and quenching effect, APTES-CQDs@MIPs were synthesized with an optimized ratio of template, functional monomer, and cross-linker.

The fluorescence measurements of APTS-CQDs and APTS-CQDs@MIPs were performed at λex = 350 nm in the emission range of 300 to 650 nm with slit width 10 nm (λex / λem), voltage 400 V, and scanning speed of 3000 nm min−1. The stock solution of metronidazole (MTZ), 2-methyl imidazole (2-MIZ), secnidazole (SNZ), dimetridazole (DMZ), ornidazole (ORZ), acetaminophen (ACP), and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) with concentration (1.0 mmol) were prepared. A certain volume of metronidazole standard solution was mixed with 5 mg of APTES-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs in 0.1 mol phosphate buffer solution (pH 6) and milli-Q to make a volume of 10 ml. After 5 min of sonication, the fluorescent measurement for APTS-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs was recorded as F0 (absence) and F (presence of MTZ template).

In this experiment, MTZ spiked water samples were used to verify the practicality of the synthesized APTS-CQDs@MIPs fluorescent probe. Water samples were collected from three different sources; tap water from Ocean University of China, river water from the Licun River Licang district of Qingdao, wastewater from Sheibi Qingdao, and seawater from the nearshore of Qingdao City. All of the samples were filtered through 0.22 μm membrane and kept at 4 °C. The standard solutions of MTZ with 3 μM, 6 μM, and 9 μM were spiked into the water samples, and the concentration of the added MTZ was determined by the standard addition method. The fluorescence emission spectra of APTES-CQDs@MIPs under λex = 350 nm were recorded. Each result is the average of three replicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of APTES-CQDs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Molecularly imprinted APTES-CQDs were synthesized as shown in Fig. 1 and the strategy can be generalized into three steps. (1) Water-dispersible APTES-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using APTES as a multi-amino silane coordinating solvent and orange peels as a carbon source. The synthesis of APTES-CQDs involved the decomposition and pyrolysis of orange peels which was accompanied by organosilane-functionalization of the surface [33]. The surface functionalization took place in the reaction of the carboxyl groups derived from the pyrolysis species and the amino groups of APTES, which enhanced the fluorescence properties, stability, and water dispersibility of APTES-CQDs and made them more appropriate for use in different applications [34]. (2) The synthesized APTES-CQDs were polymerized with MTZ by the reverse microemulsion method using APTES, TEOS, (functional monomer and cross-linker), and ammonium hydroxide. (3) Under optimal conditions, APTES-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs were synthesized and washed with methanol to elute imprinted template molecules to obtain specific binding cavities for rebinding of MTZ. The washing solution was then scanned by a UV spectrophotometer and its absorption value was determined for at least four washes as illustrated in Fig. 2 (B). The UV absorption value at 310 nm wavelength gradually dropped with increasing washing times until it nearly reached zero after 4th time washing. Fluorescence emission spectra of APTES-CQDs@NIPs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs before and after washing are shown in Fig. 2 (A).

The properties of the synthesized fluorescent materials were characterized by fluorescence, UV visible spectrophotometry, XRD, FTIR, and XPS spectroscopy. The optical properties of APTES-CQDs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs were evaluated by UV and fluorescence spectrograms. APTES-CQDs@MIPs had blue fluorescence with λex/λem at 350 and 437 nm respectively as illustrated in Fig. 2 (A-b). The absorption spectrum of APTES-CQDs was shown in Fig. 2 (C). The peaks at 270 nm and 300 nm were attributed to π–π* transition of the CC bond (transitions from the graphitic cores). The peak at 350 nm was associated with n–π* transition of -COOH or -NH2 groups (transition from surface defects) [35,36]. The absorption spectrum of APTES-CQDs@MIPs shown in Fig. 2 (C) revealed the diminished characteristics absorbance peaks of APTES-CQDs, which was caused by the coating of MIPs on the surface of APTES-CQDs.

XRD of pristine graphitic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) usually showed a strong (002) diffraction sharp peak close to 25°–26° which denotes ordered graphitic layers [37], whereas pure amorphous silica showed a broad XRD peak near 22.5° attributed to its non-crystalline SiO₂ structure [38]. The XRD spectrum of APTES-CQDs shown in Fig. 2 (D) demonstrated that the broad amorphous peak located at 2θ = 23° was attributed to the (002) graphitic carbon. This amorphous structure might be due to defects caused by oxygen-containing functional groups during the surface functionalization of CQD [39]. As shown in Fig. 2 (D) after polymerization the graphitic domains of APTES-CQDs were further fragmented due to hydrogen bonding and cross-linking with TEOS increasing interlayer spacing (d-spacing) and decreasing long-range order (structural flaws) and causing the peak broadening of APTES-CQDs@MIPs [40,41]. Both XRD and UV absorption spectra demonstrated that the synthesis and functionalization of APTES-CQDs was successful.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of APTES-CQDs, APTES-CQDs@NIPs, and APTES-CQDs@MIPs were shown in Fig. 2 (E) and their peak positions with corresponding functional groups before and after imprinting were summarized in Table 1 . In APTES-CQDs FTIR spectrum, peaks at 3350 and 3280 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration stretching of the -NH group and peaks at 2860 and 2927 cm−1 corresponded to CH stretching vibrations (asymmetric stretching of -CH2 group and symmetric stretching of -CH2 and -CH3 groups) that originate from silane chains which validate the attachment of APTES molecules to sp
3 hybridized carbons. As shown in Table 1 the weak peak at 1650 cm−1 confirmed the presence of CO, which corresponded to the absorption band of the amide linkage (−CONH) [42,43]. The peak at 1570 cm−1 was attributed to bending vibrations of NH and NH2 deformation [44]. These absorption peaks supported the effective synthesis of APTES-CQDs and demonstrated the formation of the CO and NH groups, which validated the acylation of the CQDs with APTES at high temperatures [44]. Peaks in the range of 1200–1400 cm−1 might be due to the bending of CH2 or other aliphatic hydrocarbon compounds. CC and CO stretching of carbon-based materials indicated that APTES-CQDs contain various carbon functionalities. As shown in Table 1 the peaks at 1082, 999, and 752 cm−1 were associated with asymmetric stretching of Si–O–C, Si–O–Si and SiO indicating that in situ carbonization of orange peels and hydrolysis of APTES creating a heteroatom-rich surface of -NH2, -OH/C=O, -Si-OH/Si-O-Si suggesting that APTES-CQDs were successfully synthesized [43]. Results from Table 1 demonstrates the fundamental characteristic peaks of APTES-CQDs did not change after polymerization to form APTES-CQDs@MIPs. APTES-CQDs@MIPs spectrum depicted that the peaks at 2894 and 2991 cm−1 were ascribed to CH stretching vibrations and peaks at 1558 and 1640 cm−1 were attributed to CN and CO stretching vibrations, which showed that the polymer contains amine functionalities. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 2 (E) the appearance of more prominent peaks at 1108, 1022, and 776 cm−1 were associated with asymmetric stretching of Si–O–C, Si–O–Si, and SiO absorption coupling respectively, which suggested the successful cross-linking from TEOS during polymerization. In addition, the peak at 1108 cm−1 (Si–O–C) further confirms the successful molecular imprinting process [42,44]. The FTIR spectra of APTES-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs showed identical major bands indicating that the two nanocomposites have similar chemical structures.

The Brauner-Emmett-Teller (BET) theory was used to measure the specific surface area (SSA), and pore size distributions (PSD) were calculated using Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analysis. SSA, PSD, and pore volume of APTES-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs are elucidated in Table 2 . SSA of APTES-CQDs@MIPs (66.8 m2/g) was seven times higher as compared to APTES-CQDs@NIPs (9.3 m2/g), which could be attributed to the high porosity of APTES-CQDs@MIPs as result of successful formation of specific binding sites on APTES-CQDs [45]. Moreover, higher pore volume of APTES-CQDs@MIPs (0.34 cm3/g) than APTES-CQDs@NIPs (0.029 cm3/g) indicating better well-defined porosity and greater accessibility for MTZ interaction to binding sites. Likewise, the pore diameter of APTES-CQDs@MIPs (9.25 Dv(d) nm) suggested mesoporosity ideal for efficient binding and diffusion of MTZ [46]. APTES-CQDs@MIPs exhibited enhanced SSA of 66.8 m2/g surpasses previously reported values, indicating the effective formation of specific binding sites with high porosity and potential for target molecule interaction [4,47].

To evaluate size distribution, dispersibility, and colloidal stability, APTES-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs were characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential (ZP) and summarized in Table 2. The ZP curves (ZP values) were used as benchmark, ≥±30 mV (high colloidal stability) and ≤±30 mV (less colloidal stability) [48,49]. As shown in Fig. S1 (A) the surface of APTES-CQDs@MIPs is positively charged with 54.4 ± 7.71 mV as compared to APTES-CQDs@NIPs +40.8 ± 9.13 mV given in Fig. S1 (C). APTES-CQDs@MIPs particles exhibited high colloidal stability as compared to similar reported methods [6,49]. Higher ZP of APTES-CQDs@MIPs confirmed a successful imprinting process which enhanced surface charge density on the surface of APTES-CQDs@MIPs that could be attributed to amino groups in the binding sites. APTES-CQDs@MIPs exhibited zeta potential of +54.4 mV, ensuring colloidal stability in high ionic strength aqueous medium, which are highly compatible with seawater applications and address a critical gap in environmental sensing, as most QDs combined MIPs sensors fail in saline media or have less colloidal stability.

DLS determines the particle size distribution of colloidal particles of varying sizes that have varying dispersion times in solution. DLS uses a time function to detect the intensity of scattered light from the laser that enters the particle-containing fluid [49]. To examine the aggregation state of the composite, the hydrodynamic size values were utilized as a benchmark, >100 nm (polydispersed) and <100 nm (monodispersed) [48]. The particle size distribution of APTES-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs was given in Fig. S1 (B, D). APTES-CQDs@MIPs showed a relatively uniform narrow hydrodynamic size distribution of 398.2 ± 41.3 nmas compared to APTES-CQDs@NIPs 646.2 ± 80.3 as given in Table 2.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to explore element analysis and surface composition of APTES-CQDs, APTES-CQDs@NIPs, and APTES-CQDs@MIPs. The corresponding XPS survey spectra were depicted in Fig. 3 (A-C), which exhibited four peaks O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and Si 2p, and the atomic percentages and fitted spectra of these elements were systematically summarized in Table S1. The O1s spectrum of APTES-CQDs was resolved into three singlet peaks at 530.91, 532.12, and 532.55 eV, and ascribed to CO, C–O–C/C–OH, and H–OH functional groups, respectively as shown in Fig. 3 (A) [35,50]. The N1s spectrum was disentangled into three peaks given at 398.95, 399.79 and 400.84 eV and attributed to C=N (pyridinic nitrogen), CN (pyrrolic nitrogen), and C–N–C (graphitic nitrogen) respectively, indicating that most of the amines of APTES molecules bounded by the silane groups reacted with carbon quantum dots [44,51,52]. The C1s spectrum was de-convoluted into three peaks given at 284.65, 285.80, and 287.73 eV corresponding to CC/C–C, CN, and CO respectively [50]. Two sub-peaks at 102.22 and 102.68 eV were fitted from Si 2p spectrum and were attributed to SiC and SiO respectively, confirming that the APTES-CQDs were successfully synthesized. XRD, FTIR, and XPS data collectively demonstrated the successful fabrication of APTES-CQDs possessed amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, which contributed to their exceptional water solubility of APTES-CQDs.

XPS survey and high-resolution spectra of APTES-CQDs@NIPs are shown in Fig. 3 (B). O1s spectra were split into three peaks at 531.83, 532.23, and 533.05 eV, and ascribed to CO, C–O–C/C–OH, and H–OH [35,50]. The N1s spectra were extracted into three peaks at 399.13, 400.30, and 401.32 eV, corresponding to CN, C–N–C, and NH respectively [53]. The C1s spectrum of APTES-CQDs@NIPs was further dissociated to three single peaks at 284.60, 285.77, and 287.69 eV with corresponding functional groups attributed to CC/C–C, CN, and CO respectively [50]. The Si 2p spectra further revealed two peaks at 102.49 and 103.05 eV assigned to SiC and SiO respectively [53]. The XPS survey spectra of APTES-CQDs@MIPs were deconvoluted as shown in Fig. 3 (C). O1s spectra yielded three peaks located at 531.40, 532.46, and 533.49 eV, and attributed CO, C–O/C–O–C and –COOH respectively [53,54]. As illustrated in Table S1 the differences in the binding energy of O1s spectra of APTES-CQDs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs indicated that the electronic environment surrounding the oxygen atoms has been changed and could be due to the formation of new functional groups or modifications to hybridization states brought by polymerization [55]. Characteristics peak of APTES-CQDs (CO and CO) were retained demonstrating chemical properties of the original CQDs have not been changed. The N1s spectrum was extracted into three peaks at 399.26, 400.54, and 401.56 eV, corresponding to CN, C–N–C, and NH, respectively [54,56]. The change in electronic environment and binding energy of N1s of APTES-CQDs@MIPs might be due to hydrogen bonding resulting in the appearance of NH peak at 401.56 eV confirms the MIPs polymerization on APTES-CQDs [57]. The C1s spectrum of APTES-CQDs@MIPs was further dissociated and three single peaks with corresponding functional groups given at 284.62, 285.88, and 287.82 eV were attributed to CC/C–C, CN, and CO, respectively [50]. The Si 2p spectrum yielded two peaks at 102.87 and 103.17 eV were assigned to SiC and SiO respectively [53]. The results of APTES-CQDs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs XPS survey spectra showed that both materials were successfully synthesized. In addition, total oxygen (O) and silica (Si) content rose as a result of the formation of new oxygen-containing functional groups (SiO), carbonyl groups (C=O), and hydroxyl groups (-OH) confirming the polymerization of MIPs on CQDs [58]. Results from Table S1 demonstrated that all three materials had a consistent number of resolved peaks (C, N, O, and Si elements). This uniformity suggested that all materials have comparable chemical surroundings. Higher intensities of C and N peaks in APTES-CQDs might be due to the unbound functional groups. APTES-CQDs@NIPs exhibited intermediate atomic % of C and O and higher atomic % of nitrogen as given in Table S1 suggests that there could be possibilities of retaining some functional groups and amino functionalities without specific imprinting. APTES-CQDs@MIPs exhibited changed peak intensities of C and O as a result of particular interactions and modifications to the chemical environment brought about by polymerization. APTES-CQDs@MIPs had intense Si peaks and showed an enhanced degree of polymerization and cross-linking as compared to APTES-CQDs@NIPs. Both FT-IR and XPS analysis verified the existence of -OH, -NH, and -COOH groups, validating and supporting that synthesized materials had excellent hydrophilicity and stability. The results from XPS and FTIR analysis were well matched and demonstrated that multiple O-, C-, N-, and Si-containing functional groups were present on the surface of APTES-CQDs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions APTES-CQDs and APTES-CQDs@MIPs composite
ce:section.ce:para: Our work employs first-time in-situ functionalization of biomass which reduces time, cost, and complexity. Optimized synthesis conditions of APTES-CQDs were depicted in Fig. 4 (A-E). The results demonstrate that a longer reaction time (20 h at 200 °C) enhances carbonization and functionalization efficiency compared to shorter durations. A high concentration of APTES (3 ml) ensures robust surface amine density, enabling stronger interactions. The maximum fluorescence of APTES-CQDs was obtained using 0.5 g of orange peels and 3 ml of APTES at 200 °C for 20 h. APTES acts as both a nitrogen dopant (-NH2 groups enhance fluorescence) and a silica precursor (hydrolyzes to form a protective matrix, enhancing photostability) enabling simultaneous functionalization and passivation. No prior work combines these roles in CQD synthesis.

The fluorescence spectra of APTES-CQDs with excitation wavelengths between 300 and 400 nm are shown in Fig. 4 (F). The maximum fluorescence emission peak at 435 nm under 350 nm excitation wavelength was attributed to the radiative recombination of the electrons and holes confined to their surface [59]. When the excitation wavelength is >360 nm, the emission wavelength of CQDs undergoes redshifts and the fluorescence intensity decreases. Some other studies also reported the excitation-dependent emission behavior of CQDs [60]. Different factors affecting the optical properties of APTES-CQDs i.e. concentration effect, solvent effect, pH effect, and temperature effect were discussed briefly in Supporting Material.

The ratio of template, monomer, and cross-linking agent influences the function of the sensor and hence directly impacts the fluorescence quenching ability of the sensor to detect the target analyte [61]. Different molar ratios of template (MTZ), functional monomer (APTES), and cross-linker (TEOS) were designed for molecular imprinting of APTES-CQDs, which are crucial in determining the efficiency of sensor and availability of recognition sites within the polymer matrix and summarized in Table 3 . It was found that the specified ratio of 1:4:20 was optimal. Compared to the optimal ratio 1:4:20, lower concentrations of template MTZ occupied fewer binding sites but higher concentrations occupied more binding sites, and both led to steric hindrance or saturation effects resulting in less fluorescence quenching as shown in Fig. 5 (A) [33,62]. The relationship between imprinted layer thickness and binding efficiency was also investigated to know, how it affects adsorption kinetics and selectivity. As illustrated in Table 3 lower amounts of functional monomer resulted in insufficient imprinted binding sites but excessive functional monomer would increase the thickness of the MIPs layer on the CQDs, which resulted in less favorable binding of the template molecules and less quenching [63]. In addition, higher or lower concentrations of functional monomer reduced the selectivity and increased non-specific adsorption and the thickness of the imprinting layer restricting rapid removal and rebinding of template [64]. If MIPs have a thick imprinting layer, the template molecule could be embedded deep and difficult to remove completely, and the ability of the target analyte to diffuse into and out of the dense imprinting layer was reduced, resulting in fewer binding sites and the decreasing of adsorption capacity of the sensor. On the other hand, a thin imprinting layer may result in insufficient adsorption as the template molecules cannot be completely embedded in binding cavities generated, hence lower adsorption capacity and less selectivity towards the target analyte may arise. Previous research discovered that template molecules contained in spherical MIPs with a diameter of 5.20 mm would be fully eluted, reaching the maximum binding capacity [65]. According to these findings, the imprinting occurred at a ratio of 1:4:20 maximized MTZ interaction, facilitating efficient energy transfer or charge transfer that led to higher quenching [61].

The dosage of the sensor, pH of the solution, and response time could effectively influence the sensing outcomes of the sensor. To maximize the detection ability of APTES-CQD@MIPs upon rebinding, key parameters such as pH, sensor dosage, and response time were optimized. The pH of the solution has a significant effect on the adsorption capacity of the APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor. Varied pH values (2−10) at 15 μM of MTZ were assessed as shown in Fig. S3. Under acidic conditions (pH < 4), the protonation of the binding sites of the APTES-CQDs@MIPs layer reduced the recognition ability which could weaken the electrostatic attraction between MTZ and APTES-CQDs@MIPs leading to small quenching [66]. The nitroimidazole ring of MTZ has a hydroxyl group (-OH) and a nitro group (-NO₂) that started protonated when pH >3 and at pH =6 became fully protonated and maximum fluorescence quenching occurred at pH 6 selected as optimal pH for subsequent experiments. As the pH is closer to neutral, metronidazole starts to deprotonate. Surface silica shell would be ionized and binding sites vanished in an alkaline environment (pH > 9), resulting in a decrease in fluorescence quenching [67,68]. The dosage of the APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor also affects the fluorescence quenching efficiency, so the dosage of the sensor was optimized. Fluorescence responses of different dosages of the sensor (100–700 mg l−1) were assessed (at optimized pH = 6 given in Fig. S3.) for 15 μM of MTZ concentration as shown in Fig. 5 (B). Inadequate dosage of the sensor produced fewer binding sites and less fluorescence intensity, which led to imprecise measurement outcomes. A high dosage of the sensor may lead to fluorescence self-absorption also resulting in less fluorescence detection. Therefore, the final dosage of the APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor was taken at 500 mg l−1.

The certain amount of time that is required for MTZ to reach the imprinted cavities and obtain recognition by the sensor was taken as response time. The response time of the APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor (5 mg/10 ml) with 15 μΜ MTZ concentration was investigated between 0 and 6 min as shown in Fig. 5 (C, D). After the addition of MTZ, fluorescence decreased rapidly and reached a minimum within 1 min which indicated that MTZ occupied the maximum number of binding sites and was combined sufficiently with APTES-CQDs@MIPs. Therefore 1 min was taken as the response time emphasizing the ultrafast kinetics as compared to a similar CQDs-based sensing method [69] for achieving rapid detection of MTZ in water matrices.

To demonstrate the fluorescent sensing ability of APTES-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs under optimal conditions for detecting MTZ, the quantitative fluorescence analysis was evaluated in the MTZ concentration range of 0.1–40 μM as shown in Fig. 6 . Under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the photoluminescence emission intensity of APTES-CQDs@MIPs at 437 nm decreased significantly with the increasing concentration of MTZ, showing that the imprinted binding cavities on the surface of APTES-CQDs@MIPs were occupied by MTZ. Higher sensitivity and precise concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching are shown in Fig. 6 (A). However, the fluorescence intensity of APTES-CQDs@NIPs at 437 nm decreased slightly as compared to APTES-CQDs@MIPs with the same concentration range of MTZ as shown in Fig. 6 (B), which was explained by only the existence of non-specific adsorption on the surface of APTES-CQDs@NIPs [70]. The calibration plot of F0/F was obtained against different concentrations of MTZ using the Stern-Volmer equation.

K SV
C

Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the sensor in the absence and presence of a template molecule. Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [C] is the concentration of the quenching agent (template molecule MTZ). A positive linear correlation was found over varied MTZ concentrations of 0.17–40 μM as illustrated in Fig. 6 (C), and the linear equation for APTES-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs were given as y = 0.0547x + 0.956 (R2 = 0.997) and y = 0.0117x + 0.995 (R2 = 0.994) respectively, demonstrating that APTES-CQDs@MIPs have functionality i.e. size and shape on the surface that recognize the MTZ molecules as well as the interaction between functional groups (−NH2 and − OH) in the polymeric network and MTZ. Here hydrogen bonding might be the main force in driving molecular adsorption [71]. The slope of the calibration curve (Ksv) was used to determine the quenching amount and imprinting factor (IF) of the fluorescent sensor.

The KSV value reflects the quenching efficiency of a sensor and the Ksv values of APTES-CQDs@MIPs and APTES-CQDs@NIPs were 0.0547 and 0.0117 respectively and higher Ksv values of APTES-CQDs@MIPs demonstrated that highly sensitive imprinted bindings were produced successfully on the surface of the sensor during polymerization for specific recognition of MTZ molecules. In addition, APTES-CQDs@MIPs exhibited high quenching efficiency for MTZ as compared to MIP@CDs (Ksv 0.0322) for naproxen [72] and CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs (Ksv 0.0425) for terbuthylazine [6] detection. The superior quenching efficiency of APTES-CQDs@MIPs could be attributed to the enhanced surface functionalization and the strong hydrogen bonding interactions, leading to improved binding affinity and fluorescence quenching.

The imprinting factor (IF), a ratio of Ksv
MIPs to Ksv
NIPs was calculated as 4.7 for our proposed fluorescent sensor, which was also evident that the synthesized fluorescent sensor has high selectivity and superior performance as compared to other similar reported methods [5,6]. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection of 0.05 μM was calculated with IUPC-defined 3Sb/S, where S is the slope of the calibration plot, and Sb is the standard deviation of the blank sample [32]. Unlike prior studies, the APTES-CQDs@MIPs demonstrate exceptional performance in terms of sensitivity, detection limit [73], and linear dynamic range [74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection mechanism of APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching can be broadly classified into static quenching, dynamic quenching, energy transfer, and inner filter effect [75]. The energy transfer process occurs when there is a spectral overlap between the donor and acceptor. Therefore, the UV absorption spectra of MTZ and fluorescence emission spectra of APTES-CQDs@MIPs were initially examined. Fig. 6 (D) showed that there was no spectral overlap between UV absorbance spectra of MTZ and fluorescence emission spectra of APTES-CQDs@MIPs which excluded the possibility of fluorescence energy transfer and inner filter effect [76].

As illustrated in Fig. 6 (E) the band gap of APTES-CQDs@MIPs and MTZ is close to each other suggesting that charge transfer occurred between the positively charged amino group and electron-rich regions of MTZ, particularly imidazole ring leading fluorescence quenching. In addition, the experiment was optimized at pH6 the hydrogen bonding might occur between the amino groups (-NH2) of the sensor and electron-withdrawing group (-NO2) of MTZ in the specific binding cavities. Besides the hydrogen bonding Meisenheimer complex was hypothesized to form between the negatively charged hydroxyl group (–OH) of MTZ and the positively charged amino group of the sensor.

To further study the quenching mechanism Stern-Volmer calibration plot was analyzed as shown in Fig. 6 (C) demonstrating that fluorescence intensity (F0/F) changed linearly with the increasing concentration of quencher (MTZ) concentration, therefore, it was hypothesized that quenching might be a dynamic or static process. To verify this hypothesis, fluorescence lifetime was obtained as shown in Fig. 6 (F). The fluorescence lifetimes of APTES-CQDs@MIPs in the absence and presence of MTZ did not change. This can be explained by the formation of a stable ground-state complex that would result in the quenching of certain fluorophores. Those fluorophores were not involved in complex formation, leading to no substantial change in the fluorescence lifetime of APTES-CQDs@MIPs indicating that the quenching mechanism was due to a static process [77]. In summary, all the results collectively evidence that the observed quenching mechanism between APTES-CQDs@MIPs and MTZ was due to charge/electron transfer and static process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and specificity of APTES-CQDs@MIPs for MTZ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the APTES-CQDs@MIPs was assessed by analyzing fluorescence response to structural analogs and co-existing metal ions. Chemicals like ornidazole (ORZ), dimetridazole (DMZ), secnidazole (SNZ) and 2-methyl imidazole (2-MIZ) were referenced as structural analogs. O-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and acetaminophen (ACP) were other reference substances. Their structure and properties are summarized in Table S2. The selectivity and recognition ability of APTES-CQDs@MIPs were investigated for these chemicals at their concentration of 15 μM. The fluorescence quenching of MTZ was significantly higher than all analogs as shown in Fig. 7 (A1–3). ORZ and DMZ showed slightly more quenching among analogs, which can be explained by their comparable structure (imidazole ring and nitro group) to metronidazole, enabling π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions with the imprinted sites in the polymer. As compared to analogs of ORZ and DMZ, SNZ has a methoxy group and showed less quenching due to steric effects. 2-MIZ (lack of nitro group), o-PD and ACP (lack of specific functional groups) exhibited noticeably less interaction with APTES-CQDs@MIPs. The order of reactivity in terms of fluorescence quenching was as MTZ > ORZ > DMZ > SNZ > 2-MIZ > o-PD > ACP, and the structural analogs did not affect the detection of APTES-CQDs@MIPs for MTZ. The tightly binding of MTZ to APTES-CQDs@MIPs resulted in a reduction in luminescence intensity. Different ionic species are usually present in aqueous samples [78]. So different metal ions were tested as main interferents with concentrations ten times higher than MTZ (150 μM) and showed little influence on the detection of MTZ as shown in Table S3.

No discernible change in the fluorescence intensity was caused by these metal ions as shown in Fig. 7 (B1–3). The fluorescence of both fluorescent materials APTE-CQDs@MIPs and APTE-CQDs@NIPs slightly quenched by the presence of combined common ions with a similar degree, which was attributed to non-specific adsorption of ions on the surface of both fluorescent materials [77]. The combining of analogs and ions with MTZ also did not impact the fluorescence intensity of MTZ as shown in Fig. 7 (C1–3) despite the higher concentration used than their low concentration as ng·l−1 in natural water. Larger concentrations of these interferences did not affect the selectivity of our proposed sensor to detect lower concentrations of MTZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, recyclability, and reproducibility of APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of APTES-CQDs@MIPs was assessed over a period to verify the effectiveness of the fluorescent sensor. The fluorescence intensity of the sensor was measured between 2 and 10 h as a short-term stability (intraday) shown in Fig. 8 (A). Long-term stability (interday) was also measured every 4 days for 20 days under the same conditions as shown in Fig. 8 (B). The fluorescence intensity of APTES-CQDs@MIPs did not change significantly, demonstrating its exceptional short-term and long-term stability. One important feature that greatly improves the sustainability and efficiency of sensing applications is recyclability. The good recyclability of fluorescent sensors will increase their cost-effectiveness and longevity, making them useful for long-term monitoring and analysis without compromising sensitivity or selectivity by repeating sensing cycles. The recyclability of the synthesized APTES-CQDs@MIPs was evaluated by the same elution procedure after APTES-CQDs@MIPs were used. The template molecules were eluted using methanol solvent followed by centrifugation, and then APTES-CQDs@MIPs were dried to use again. After 4 cycles the fluorescence quenching performance of the APTES-CQDs@MIPs slightly decreased by 10.1 % as compared to the first cycle with RSD 3.37 % as shown in Fig. 8 (C) [4,79] demonstrating the robustness and stability of sensor, making it suitable for repeated use in practical applications. Reproducibility is an essential property for the widespread usage of fluorescent APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensors, which was examined by measuring the fluorescence quenching for four different batches of sensors. The result showed that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of APTES-CQDs@MIPs did not change substantially for these batches as shown in Fig. 8 (D), yielding a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.45 %. The sensor exhibited outstanding reproducibility. According to the above experimental findings, the synthesized APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor with good stability, recyclability, and reproducibility can facilitate a variety of practical detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of APTES-CQDs@MIPs to real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: MTZ in tap, river, waste, and seawater with spiked concentrations of 0, 3, 6 and 9 μM was measured by the developed APTES-CQDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor as shown in Table S4. MTZ was not detected in actual water samples before spiking and recoveries of spiked samples were obtained by the sensor in the range from 95.1 % to 108.2 % with RSD of 0.86 % to 2.19 %. The comparison between the developed sensor with standard HPLC and other reported methods for the detection of MTZ in different matrixes is shown in Table S5. In spite fact that HPLC has low detection limits and good sensitivity, our proposed sensor showed comparable recovery and RSD as well as high sensitivity and selectivity. APTES-CQDs@MIPs eliminate the need for toxic solvents and reduce the cost. Fast response time makes it preferable over HPLC. In addition, APTES-CQDs@MIPs showed good performance in terms of sensitivity, wide dynamic range (0.17–40 μM), and detection limit (0.05 μM) as compared to other reported methods [68,73]. The validation parameters are summarized in Table S6 for the developed fluorescent method suggested that the APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor has the advantages of high sensitivity, selectivity, low cost, and rapid test ability. It is a reliable approach for the determination of metronidazole and offers substantial eco-green detection applications. Moreover, the molecular imprinting technique used in this experiment improved the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the method.

The current study developed a novel, simple, rapid, and eco-green APTES-CQDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor for the detection of MTZ in the aqueous environment. APTES-CQDs were firstly synthesized with a cost-effective hydrothermal technique using orange peels as a carbon source and APTES as a surface functionalization agent at optimized ratio and conditions. The synthesized APTES-CQDs had high water solubility and stability with excitation-dependent bright blue photoluminescence. The APTES-CQDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor with enhanced selectivity and sensitivity was then synthesized by using reverse microemulsion with optimized ratio and conditions for rapid and accurate detection of MTZ in water samples. The developed APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor showed remarkable sensitivity and selectivity towards MTZ in the presence of analogs and interferants and achieved good stability, recyclability, and reproducibility. Charge/electron transfer and static quenching occurred between MTZ and APTES-CQDs@MIPs sensor and were responsible for the fluorescence quenching. The constructed APTES-CQDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor can efficiently detect MTZ in aqueous samples rapidly and precisely, and expand the application of biomass-based APTES-CQDs in the various sensing fields.
